title: Help! My Dog Licked a Laundry Detergent Pod: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog in danger after licking a laundry pod? Learn why detergent pods are high-risk emergencies and what steps you need to take immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Laundry detergent ingestion is a high-priority emergency that requires immediate veterinary care due to the risk of chemical burns and life-threatening aspiration pneumonia.

What happens when my dog eats or punctures a laundry detergent pod?

Laundry detergent pods contain highly concentrated surfactants and chemicals designed to strip stains. When a dog licks or punctures a pod, these concentrated chemicals can cause immediate chemical burns or severe irritation to the mouth, esophagus, and stomach. Because the liquid inside is often under pressure, it can spray into the back of the throat or be inhaled into the lungs.

Is laundry detergent ingestion a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: High. You should treat this as an immediate emergency. Even a small lick can cause significant distress.
  • Aspiration Pneumonia Risk: The greatest danger is the dog inhaling soapy foam into their lungs, which can be fatal.
  • Do Not Induce Vomiting: Never attempt to induce vomiting at home, as the bubbling action of the detergent can cause your dog to inhale the chemicals, leading to life-threatening lung inflammation.
  • Seek Help: Contact a veterinarian or pet poison control immediately.

Why should I take a photo of the laundry detergent packaging for the vet?

  • Identify Surfactants: Different brands use different concentrations of ionic and anionic surfactants.
  • Detect Additional Hazards: The label confirms if the product contains extra hazardous ingredients like bleach or specific fabric softeners.
  • Optimize Treatment: Seeing the packaging helps the vet determine the exact toxicity level and choose the most effective rinsing and treatment protocols.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of laundry detergent, clinical signs include nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, potentially leading to secondary dehydration and electrolyte imbalance. Mild ocular irritation with lacrimation and blepharospasm may also occur. Treatment involves dilution with milk or water to reduce the risk of spontaneous vomiting. If vomiting is not self-limiting, antiemetics such as metoclopramide (0.2-0.4 mg/kg, PO, SC, or IM, qid) may be administered. In rare instances, parenteral fluid therapy is required. Vomiting can be induced within 30 minutes to 2 hours after ingestion using 3% hydrogen peroxide (2.2 mL/kg, up to 45 mL, repeated once after 10-15 minutes if needed) or apomorphine hydrochloride (0.03 mg/kg, IV, once; or a crushed tablet dissolved in saline instilled into the conjunctival sac and rinsed away after emesis). Activated charcoal (2 g/kg, PO, repeated every 6-8 hours) may be administered to reduce toxin absorption if the animal is conscious. Affected areas should be flushed with tepid water or physiologic saline and monitored for dermal or ocular irritation or ulceration. Topical treatment for dermal or ocular burns should be instituted as needed; in severe cases, analgesics may be indicated.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3004)

faqs.question: What are the immediate symptoms of detergent poisoning?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive drooling, pawing at the mouth, gagging, persistent vomiting, and coughing or labored breathing.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog water or milk?
faqs.answer: You may offer a small amount of water or milk to help rinse the mouth, but only if your dog is fully conscious and not coughing. Never force liquids.

faqs.question: What will the vet do for my dog?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely flush the mouth and eyes, monitor the airway, and may perform X-rays to check for signs of aspiration pneumonia or lung irritation.
